data.title: Institutional investors have a lot riding on Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust Limited (LON:SREI) with 48% ownership
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Insights

  • Given the large stake in the stock by institutions, Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust's stock price might be vulnerable to their trading decisions
  • 51% of the business is held by the top 10 shareholders
  • Using data from company's past performance alongside ownership research, one can better assess the future performance of a company

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

A look at the shareholders of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust Limited (LON:SREI) can tell us which group is most powerful. With 48% stake, institutions possess the maximum shares in the company. Put another way, the group faces the maximum upside potential (or downside risk).

Last week’s 15% gain means that institutional investors were on the positive end of the spectrum even as the company has shown strong longer-term trends. One-year return to shareholders is currently 16% and last week’s gain was the icing on the cake.

In the chart below, we zoom in on the different ownership groups of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust.

View our latest analysis for Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust

What Does The Institutional Ownership Tell Us About Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust?

Institutions typically measure themselves against a benchmark when reporting to their own investors, so they often become more enthusiastic about a stock once it's included in a major index. We would expect most companies to have some institutions on the register, especially if they are growing.

We can see that Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust does have institutional investors; and they hold a good portion of the company's stock. This suggests some credibility amongst professional investors. But we can't rely on that fact alone since institutions make bad investments sometimes, just like everyone does. It is not uncommon to see a big share price drop if two large institutional investors try to sell out of a stock at the same time. So it is worth checking the past earnings trajectory of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust, (below). Of course, keep in mind that there are other factors to consider, too.

Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust is not owned by hedge funds. Our data shows that Schroder Investment Management Limited is the largest shareholder with 12% of shares outstanding. In comparison, the second and third largest shareholders hold about 9.9% and 8.5% of the stock.

On further inspection, we found that more than half the company's shares are owned by the top 10 shareholders, suggesting that the interests of the larger shareholders are balanced out to an extent by the smaller ones.

Researching institutional ownership is a good way to gauge and filter a stock's expected performance. The same can be achieved by studying analyst sentiments. As far as we can tell there isn't analyst coverage of the company, so it is probably flying under the radar.

Insider Ownership Of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust

The definition of company insiders can be subjective and does vary between jurisdictions. Our data reflects individual insiders, capturing board members at the very least. Management ultimately answers to the board. However, it is not uncommon for managers to be executive board members, especially if they are a founder or the CEO.

Insider ownership is positive when it signals leadership are thinking like the true owners of the company. However, high insider ownership can also give immense power to a small group within the company. This can be negative in some circumstances.

Our most recent data indicates that insiders own less than 1% of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust Limited. It has a market capitalization of just UK£270m, and the board has only UK£184k worth of shares in their own names. Many investors in smaller companies prefer to see the board more heavily invested. You can click here to see if those insiders have been buying or selling.

General Public Ownership

The general public, who are usually individual investors, hold a 45% stake in Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust. This size of ownership, while considerable, may not be enough to change company policy if the decision is not in sync with other large shareholders.

Public Company Ownership

It appears to us that public companies own 7.0% of Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust. It's hard to say for sure but this suggests they have entwined business interests. This might be a strategic stake, so it's worth watching this space for changes in ownership.

Next Steps:

While it is well worth considering the different groups that own a company, there are other factors that are even more important. Take risks for example - Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust has 3 warning signs (and 1 which is a bit unpleasant) we think you should know about.

data.title: Is There Opportunity in Carpenter Technology After Shares Rise 26% on Aerospace Momentum?
data.description:
If you’ve been keeping an eye on Carpenter Technology lately, you know it’s been a wild ride. With the share price up 26.1% just this past week and nearly doubling over the last year, it’s no wonder investors are buzzing. The rally isn’t just recent either; over the past five years, Carpenter Technology has delivered a staggering 1,772.8% return. Moves like these are usually reserved for hyped tech stocks, yet here we are with a materials company making headline-worthy moves.

What’s fueling this action? Lately, the news has highlighted Carpenter Technology’s growing role in supplying advanced materials for the booming aerospace sector, particularly as airlines and manufacturers catch up with post-pandemic demand. The company has also attracted attention due to increased orders from the defense and energy industries, sectors that tend to get investors excited about long-term growth and stability. There’s a sense that Carpenter Technology is sitting at the crossroads of multiple megatrends, and market sentiment reflects a shift toward optimism about its possibilities.

But with stocks, excitement doesn’t always translate to value. Even with all this momentum, when we crunched the numbers using six different valuation checks, Carpenter Technology didn’t score as undervalued on any of them, giving it a valuation score of 0 out of 6. Figuring out what that really means, and whether the current price still holds opportunity, takes a closer look at the most common ways investors judge value. Let’s walk through those classic valuation approaches. At the very end, I’ll share an often-overlooked perspective that could change how you see Carpenter Technology’s stock altogether.

Carpenter Technology scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Carpenter Technology Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model projects a company’s expected future cash flows and discounts them back to today’s value, aiming to estimate what the business is really worth right now. For Carpenter Technology, this approach was based on a two-stage free cash flow to equity model, which means the analysis looked at cash flows anticipated over several years, then applied a long-term growth estimate for the company’s later years.

According to recent data, Carpenter Technology generated $323.3 million in free cash flow over the last twelve months. Analyst estimates cover five years ahead, then Simply Wall St extends those projections further. By 2030, Carpenter’s free cash flow is expected to reach $359 million, according to these forecasts. The model weighs each future year’s contribution less heavily as projections extend further out, giving a present-day value of these cash flows.

After crunching the numbers, the DCF method puts Carpenter Technology’s intrinsic value at $126.45 per share. However, the stock is currently priced 137.5% above this calculation, suggesting the market is taking a much more bullish view than the DCF does.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Carpenter Technology.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Carpenter Technology may be overvalued by 137.5%. Find undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Approach 2: Carpenter Technology Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Carpenter Technology, the Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is one of the most popular metrics investors use to assess whether a stock is reasonably valued. The PE ratio reflects how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of earnings, and it tends to be higher when growth prospects are strong and risk is perceived to be low. Conversely, a higher risk profile or dimmer growth outlook generally means a lower fair PE is justified.

Currently, Carpenter Technology trades at a PE ratio of 39.83x. When we compare that to the Aerospace and Defense industry average of 39.98x and a peer average of 33.80x, the company's multiple is roughly in line with its sector but slightly higher than its closest peers. However, benchmarks like these do not always tell the full story because they miss key details, such as unique growth rates or risk factors specific to each company.

This is where Simply Wall St's “Fair Ratio” comes into play. Their fair PE ratio for Carpenter Technology is 32.20x, a figure that reflects not only industry trends but also the company’s specific earnings growth, margins, risks, and even market capitalization. By considering these variables, the Fair Ratio offers a more nuanced baseline for valuation compared to straightforward industry or peer comparisons.

On balance, Carpenter’s PE of 39.83x is noticeably higher than the fair value assessment of 32.20x. This suggests the stock is trading at a premium to what would be reasonable based on a holistic view of its fundamentals and prospects.

Result: OVERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Carpenter Technology Narrative

Earlier, we mentioned that there’s an even better way to understand valuation, so let’s introduce you to Narratives. In investing, a Narrative is more than just a set of numbers. It is the compelling story and reasoning that connects what you believe about a company’s future, the forecasts for its revenue, earnings, and profit margins, and, ultimately, your own estimate of the stock’s fair value.

Narratives take everything you know about a business, such as industry trends, upcoming catalysts, and potential risks, and tie them directly to a financial forecast. This approach lets you clearly see how your view leads to a specific valuation. On Simply Wall St’s Community page, millions of investors use Narratives to share and update their perspectives as news breaks or earnings are released, so each Narrative is always evolving with fresh information.

data.title: Is Cellnex Telecom (BME:CLNX) A Risky Investment?
data.description:
Legendary fund manager Li Lu (who Charlie Munger backed) once said, 'The biggest investment risk is not the volatility of prices, but whether you will suffer a permanent loss of capital.' So it seems the smart money knows that debt - which is usually involved in bankruptcies - is a very important factor, when you assess how risky a company is. Importantly, Cellnex Telecom, S.A. (BME:CLNX) does carry debt. But the real question is whether this debt is making the company risky.

Advertisement

Why Does Debt Bring Risk?

Generally speaking, debt only becomes a real problem when a company can't easily pay it off, either by raising capital or with its own cash flow. Part and parcel of capitalism is the process of 'creative destruction' where failed businesses are mercilessly liquidated by their bankers. However, a more common (but still painful) scenario is that it has to raise new equity capital at a low price, thus permanently diluting shareholders. Having said that, the most common situation is where a company manages its debt reasonably well - and to its own advantage. When we think about a company's use of debt, we first look at cash and debt together.

How Much Debt Does Cellnex Telecom Carry?

The chart below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that Cellnex Telecom had €18.8b in debt in June 2025; about the same as the year before. However, it also had €1.46b in cash, and so its net debt is €17.3b.

How Strong Is Cellnex Telecom's Balance Sheet?

The latest balance sheet data shows that Cellnex Telecom had liabilities of €3.65b due within a year, and liabilities of €24.7b falling due after that. On the other hand, it had cash of €1.46b and €1.07b worth of receivables due within a year. So it has liabilities totalling €25.8b more than its cash and near-term receivables, combined.

Given this deficit is actually higher than the company's massive market capitalization of €20.0b, we think shareholders really should watch Cellnex Telecom's debt levels, like a parent watching their child ride a bike for the first time. Hypothetically, extremely heavy dilution would be required if the company were forced to pay down its liabilities by raising capital at the current share price.

View our latest analysis for Cellnex Telecom

We measure a company's debt load relative to its earnings power by looking at its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and by calculating how easily its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) cover its interest expense (interest cover). Thus we consider debt relative to earnings both with and without depreciation and amortization expenses.

Weak interest cover of 0.80 times and a disturbingly high net debt to EBITDA ratio of 7.2 hit our confidence in Cellnex Telecom like a one-two punch to the gut. The debt burden here is substantial. Looking on the bright side, Cellnex Telecom boosted its EBIT by a silky 51% in the last year. Like the milk of human kindness that sort of growth increases resilience, making the company more capable of managing debt. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But ultimately the future profitability of the business will decide if Cellnex Telecom can strengthen its balance sheet over time. So if you're focused on the future you can check out this free report showing analyst profit forecasts.

Finally, a business needs free cash flow to pay off debt; accounting profits just don't cut it. So the logical step is to look at the proportion of that EBIT that is matched by actual free cash flow. In the last three years, Cellnex Telecom created free cash flow amounting to 2.2% of its EBIT, an uninspiring performance. That limp level of cash conversion undermines its ability to manage and pay down debt.

Our View

To be frank both Cellnex Telecom's net debt to EBITDA and its track record of covering its interest expense with its EBIT make us rather uncomfortable with its debt levels. But at least it's pretty decent at growing its EBIT; that's encouraging. Overall, it seems to us that Cellnex Telecom's balance sheet is really quite a risk to the business. So we're almost as wary of this stock as a hungry kitten is about falling into its owner's fish pond: once bitten, twice shy, as they say. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. For instance, we've identified 1 warning sign for Cellnex Telecom that you should be aware of.

If, after all that, you're more interested in a fast growing company with a rock-solid balance sheet, then check out our list of net cash growth stocks without delay.

data.title: Is Hershey Fairly Priced After Recent 2.8% Drop?
data.description:
If you’ve ever had a tough time deciding what to do with Hershey stock, you’re far from alone. Whether you’re a seasoned investor or just like to keep tabs on your favorite chocolate maker, recent price swings have given everyone something to talk about. Over the past week, Hershey shares slipped 2.8%, deepening a 30-day decline of nearly 5%. Even so, the stock is holding onto a solid 7.6% gain year-to-date, and for those in it for the long haul, the five-year return stands at a robust 48%. That is not bad for a company in the sweet spot of consumer brands.

Of course, a few things have been on investors’ minds lately. The shifting landscape for consumer staples, weather impacts on cocoa supply, and recent headlines around changing consumer tastes have all had a hand in nudging sentiment. While these factors haven’t sparked any dramatic news moves, they do shape how both risk and growth are being measured in the stock.

Now, when it comes to the all-important question of whether Hershey is undervalued, the current data paint a clear picture. Based on six key valuation checks, Hershey actually scores a 0, signaling that the market isn’t missing any hidden value, at least by traditional measures. But valuation is never as simple as just ticking boxes. So, let’s dig into how these checks stack up, and stick around for the end, where we’ll look at an approach that might give you an even better read on what Hershey is really worth.

Hershey scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Hershey Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company's value by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to their present value. This approach focuses on how much cash Hershey is expected to generate each year, taking into account both near-term analyst forecasts and longer-term projections.

For Hershey, the DCF model starts with last year's Free Cash Flow (FCF) of $1.44 billion and builds forward. By year five, analysts see annual FCF reaching around $1.84 billion. Beyond these years, future cash flows are extrapolated, assuming smaller annual changes. The cash flows in later years gradually increase but at a more modest pace, all measured in US dollars.

Once all these cash flows are projected and discounted back to today's value, Hershey's intrinsic worth is estimated at $168.63 per share. When compared with the current market price, the DCF model suggests the stock is roughly 7.7% above its fair value, meaning it is more or less in line without a major discount or premium.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Hershey.

Simply Wall St performs a valuation analysis on every stock in the world every day (check out Hershey's valuation analysis). We show the entire calculation in full. You can track the result in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted when this changes.

Approach 2: Hershey Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Hershey, the Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a widely accepted measure of valuation. It tells investors how much they are paying today for a dollar of the company’s current earnings. Generally, a higher PE suggests that investors expect more growth or see lower risk, while a lower PE may signal the opposite or the presence of other concerns.

Hershey’s current PE stands at 24x, which is noticeably above the food industry average of 18x and also ahead of its main peer group’s average of 19x. On the surface, this may look expensive, but raw comparison does not take into account Hershey’s particular business strengths or future prospects. Growth expectations, stability, and risk profile all play a role in what makes a “fair” PE for any given company.

That is where Simply Wall St’s proprietary “Fair Ratio” comes in. Unlike a standard industry or peer comparison, the Fair Ratio is tailor-made, factoring in Hershey’s growth outlook, profitability, risk, industry setting, and even its market cap. In Hershey’s case, the Fair Ratio is 22.1x, suggesting this is the multiple that fairly reflects its specific prospects and risks.

With Hershey trading at 24x, compared to a Fair Ratio of 22.1x, the stock is only marginally above its fair value based on earnings and is well within a reasonable range. The judgment here, just like with the DCF approach, is that the stock’s valuation is about right.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Hershey Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let’s introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is simply your story about a company: your unique perspective on what you think will happen and why, paired with your own estimates for future revenue, earnings, and margins. Narratives take the numbers behind an investment and tie them directly to a company’s outlook, connecting those figures to a fair value that reflects your own beliefs.

On Simply Wall St’s platform, Narratives are available in the Community page, making it easy for anyone to build, share, and compare investment perspectives. No advanced modeling is required. With this tool, you can see how your fair value assessment stacks up next to others' and instantly know if the current price is attractive for your view or not. Narratives automatically update as key data and news evolve, so your investment thinking is always current.

For Hershey, some investors have a bullish Narrative, focusing on the company’s innovation pipeline and pricing power, leading them to a $211 fair value. Narratives help you see these perspectives side by side, so you can decide when to act based on your own conviction and the numbers behind it.
